introduction.content: Welcome to the I Can Study program. I'm Dr. Justin Sung, the co-founder and head of learning here. And I know that right now, if you're anything like I used to be, you probably want to get straight into learning about the techniques. And I will teach you the techniques, but there's something even more important than the techniques that I wish I had known when I was first starting off on my journey. The truth is... There is no such thing as the best studying technique. Back when I was trying to enter into medical school, I thought about learning as just a series of techniques. And so being a good learner just meant having a better technique. So what I would end up doing is just picking up these new methods of writing notes or doing flashcards. And the good news is that I did manage to enter into medical school. The bad news is that I had to sacrifice a lot. I was studying 20 hours a day, seven days a week, extremely sleep deprived. I was mentally shattered, emotionally just very fragile. I had, I entered into a severe depression. which actually took me over three years to recover from. And needless to say, I was completely, completely burnt out, beyond burnt out. But some of you might be thinking that you will gladly go through that sacrifice if it means that you're going to achieve your goals. And I can admire if you are that motivated. But here's what I wish someone had told me, which is that if that's how you're thinking, You shouldn't think that way. I did not successfully enter medical school because I worked so hard and because of what I did. I... got lucky. I have friends that did just as much. I got in, they didn't. And I remember being in medical school and I'm just surrounded by like incredibly intelligent people. And I was looking down at... five years of medical school, realizing after the first week of lectures in medical school, that this was way harder than the previous year. Like if I was studying 20 hours a day beforehand, there's no way I can do like, what, 40 hours a day now in medical school. So I had to find a way to get through that. And also, have a life, not just study. I didn't want to spend the best years of my life just locked away in a library. And that's actually what it felt like sometimes. It took me easily over 10,000 hours spread across five years of constant trial and error and experimentation and reading through research to figure this out. often very confusing research. Which brings me to another point, which is that learning to become a really efficient learner is not this roadmap, the secret that has kind of been fully researched and figured out and like, it's just here. Even if you look at the learning science research that's being published today, and you look at what they say are like the most effective techniques, and you look at the stats, They say it's like 50 to 60% of the people found it pretty effective. For me, I left my job as a medical doctor to do this kind of work full time. After spending, after sacrificing so much to enter into medical school and to get through medical school and the sacrifice continually, why did I leave that? to pursue this, being a learning coach. A learning coach is not even a profession that existed. I'll be honest, the first, the reason I initially got into coaching was just because I wanted to make some money while I was going through medical school. Like, you know, I got into medical school, I did a bit of tutoring, I liked teaching, I was passionate about it. And what I quickly realized was that the students that are doing well keep doing well and the students that are struggling are still struggling. It's just now they're wasting money paying me to tutor them on things. But the real issue is why did they even need me to tutor them in the first place? During this time, I was exploring the learning science a lot. I was starting to really get into that idea of like, what does it mean to be an efficient learner? And so I was learning a lot about myself and how to learn effectively. And I started teaching this to my students that I was tutoring at the time. And then a couple of years in, I decided to create a nonprofit for trying to teach these skills without the subject tutoring. I thought, well, I really feel like 99% of the difference is made through just having the skills. So maybe I can just teach the skills directly. So I created this free mentoring program that we would run in high school. for students from lower socioeconomic backgrounds. And this is actually how I ended up gaining so much experience with learning coaching because I was working with so many students from high school through to university, through to graduation, just teaching them these skills and seeing how they found it and how it affects their life and their ability to succeed. And the powerful thing I've realized, which is what helped convince me to move away from medicine, was that I realized that this thing of learning to learn is not just like, it's not just a skill. It's not just like, hey, here's how you can study. Our ability to learn defines what we believe we can achieve in life. There is so much of ourselves and our identity that we place into how smart we think we are. And a lot of that is based on just our academic performance. And I would work with these incredible, intelligent, like... really like inspirational young students who had so much potential, but they thought of themselves as someone that just can't do this. I can't be a doctor. I can't, you know, be an engineer. I can't do this because my school grades have said that I can't do that. But you are more than your exam result. Your exam performance is, does not determine your worth as a person. Whether you do well or badly in an exam, it doesn't mean you are a better or a worse person. Intelligence is not something that is fixed. It isn't something that's just assigned to you at birth and then you're like bound to it by destiny. Intelligence is... just a series of thinking habits that can be unlearned and relearned and then trained. And because your intelligence is basically just a skill that can be trained, a series of habits, it means that your exams and your past performance are not a reflection of who you are or what you're capable of. They're a reflection of what you did at the time. And helping people realize this and make that transformation, for me at least, gave me more fulfillment and satisfaction than I had treating a debilitating disease as a medical doctor, for me at least. But that is just my story. The iCanStudy team is more than just myself. But we are bound by the common thread that we all resonate with the message that I've just said. We are all educators with decades of experience under our belts. And while each one of us has our own individual story, we were all brought together by the common realization that people need to learn these skills and that the way that it is often taught in the mainstream is not enough. So that's why this program exists, because we wanted to give you these skills that can help you feel this way and have this transformation and not just give you the confidence that you can walk into an exam and do well, but that you can achieve whatever goal that you want and that you have the confidence to go out and really try for that goal. We want you to feel like you can learn anything and do anything because we know you can. And don't get me wrong, hard work is still necessary. introduction.content: To start you off, I want to teach you something that I call the golden rule of learning. And I call it the golden rule because it's something that I've seen consistently in our top performing learners. And it's also repeated countless times in the research. But despite that, it's also something that I've noticed most people don't seem to never really think about ever, which directly makes it harder and more stressful to reach your goals. I'm going to teach this golden rule to you now upfront, because I want you to remember this for the rest of your life. For everything you learn, for everything we teach you in every lesson that follows this, I want this to be there in your mind. The golden rule is to ruthlessly focus on your locus of control. The locus of control is a term commonly used in psychology research. And in short, it means the stuff that you can control to get the outcome that you want. And when it comes to learning to learn, this is crucial. Let's say you've got this really difficult exam. that you wanna do well in. There are so many factors that influence your chance of successfully doing well in this exam. It isn't just as simple as study more and you'll do well. Yes, effort is one of those factors, but if you study lots using bad techniques, then you probably not gonna do as well as someone studying lots and using good techniques. And even outside of studying, things like your level of focus, how much you procrastinate or how easily you get distracted. All of these things influence our ability to reach our goals. And here's the unfortunate reality. We can't control all of those things. All we can do is put ourselves in the best position to succeed, to give ourselves the best chance possible. So focusing on the locus of control means that we're thinking about what are the things that we can actually do that we can control that puts us in that best position. When we don't focus on this locus of control, then we spend a lot of time trying to figure out how to achieve the goal. We spend a lot of time getting distracted, trying all sorts of different things. We're very busy, we're very worried, and we waste valuable time and effort. And so this goal we're trying to reach stays difficult and far away. Trying to reach it is risky. And so you can see here, our focus is very scattered. Whereas when we are focusing on the locus of control, it means that for the same amount of effort that we put in, we can make more productive, in fact, a surprising amount of progress towards our goals. So here's how I want you to think about achieving your goals from now on. Think about that goal that you want to achieve. It might be an exam score, entering into a degree, navigating through a job, your dream outcome. Think about that. You cannot control that. Recognize that outcome is not within your locus of control. Outcomes cannot be controlled. Just because you really want to succeed doesn't mean you will. Otherwise, The more you worry, the more you win. But I'm sure you've had situations in the past where you did really want an outcome and you were worried about it and you didn't get the outcome. Outcomes are a symptom of the process. And it sounds scary. I know. but we have to feel it and we have to accept it. Because only when we've realized that that's not something we can control, do we free ourselves up to then turn our attention to the things that we can control, AKA the processes that we use. And when the processes are right, and we will teach you those processes, then it means you've got the best chance to succeed. You're in that best position. And it may not mean that your chance of success is 100%. It probably won't be, but it's as good as it gets. And that rest, the uncertainty, the what ifs, That's just life. And we have to learn to be able to deal with that and be okay with that. So with that being said, in the next video, I'll go over how we should start thinking about these processes themselves. What is the right process? And then how can we start engaging in the right processes to upgrade the way that we learn?

introduction.content: So we want to get better grades, or we want to be better at using our knowledge for work, and we want to remember more of what we learned. These are the outcomes. And remember, outcomes are not the things we control. So what are the processes that lead to those outcomes? Well, there is a massive amount of research on this topic, and I've spent years going through all of this stuff so that you don't have to. And there are a couple of interesting things that I realized about this. The first thing, which was interesting to me, is that the actual processes themselves are actually not the hardest part. Yes, it's not straightforward always. Some of the techniques and processes do require diligent practice. But in my years of coaching and looking at who are the students that are successful and who are the ones that are not, remember how I said even in the latest research, even the most successful techniques and processes the success rate of those is only like 50 to 60%. And those are the most successful. Like your average typical learning effective technique that's been researched out there, most of the stats are showing like 20, 30% effect. And the thing that clicked for me and seeing it with my own eyes and the students that I was working with noticing what are the really successful students doing that all the other students are consistently not. I realized that there are two very fundamental perspectives about learning that you have to have before you even start learning any techniques or processes in the first place. And the first and what I think is actually the most fundamental perspective that you need to have is to think about learning in systems rather than techniques. As I said, there's no such thing as the best technique. Every technique has pros and cons and strengths and limitations. And the idea is that you use a technique that has a certain benefit in some angle for some topic and you use that and then you balance the weaknesses with a different technique. And it's about layering these techniques in the right way to create an entire system that produces an effective learner. If you just use one technique or like a couple techniques and you use them perfectly, if those two techniques have the same gap between them, you're actually not going to do very well. If you only use one thing and you use lots of it, doing more of the same thing doesn't reduce the gaps. Like the gaps are still there. And... having real confidence in your learning ability is actually about understanding what are the strengths and limitations and pros and cons of each of these techniques and how do I layer them together to create that system? Because it means that if something changes in your environment, the challenge, the difficulty of an exam, the amount of time availability that you have, your specific weakness with regards to that topic, You can think about these variables and then you can look at the techniques you're using and you can adapt it. You can mold it. You become more flexible. That's what gives you real confidence because you know, regardless of the challenge, you can make it work. So the analogy I often say is to think about learning like a toolkit, like you're building a house. It doesn't matter how good your hammer is or your screwdriver or your drill. It's not about having the best of each thing. It's about having a comprehensive toolkit where you know the right way to use each tool. So for learning, each of us already comes to the table with a toolkit, a series of learning habits that we've picked up. Some of the tools work and we keep them or we upgrade them and make them even better. Some of the tools are getting a little outdated. They need a bit of maintenance or refreshing. And some of the tools are just, we don't need them at all. And we should just get rid of them because it's just making our toolkit heavier. And that is in a nutshell, basically what we're going to be doing in this program. We're going to be going through your toolkit, showing you how to think about a toolkit, what you need in your toolkit, what the best tool for each part is in that toolkit, helping you examine what you've got and then helping you remove and replace it. So that's the first perspective. You have to think about learning in systems rather than in techniques. Otherwise, you're not going to make a lot of good progress with learning to learn. The second perspective is to open the black box. Think about this. If you are racing a car, and you want to be able to make that car go as fast as possible, you need to be able to open up that hood and know what you're looking at. The exact same thing is true for learning, except the engine of the car is our brain.
